coredata.dc:title: Excellent anti-corrosion performance of epoxy composite coatings filled with novel N-doped carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shaoxiang
Du, Feifan
Lin, Yanxu
Guan, Yu
Qu, WenJuan
Cheng, Jiaji
Wang, Dong
coredata.dc:description: Nanofillers play an important role in the field of anticorrosion. This paper focuses on the study of the anticorrosion performance of a novel N-doped carbon nanodots(CNDs) in epoxy coating. N-doped carbon nanodots(CNDs) with a size of about 100 nm is synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid as carbon source. The chemical structure and morphology of CNDs are studied by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FT-IR), ultraviolet spectrophotometer(UV–vis), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS), scanning electron microscopy(SEM) and transmission electron microscopy(TEM). The water absorption of the coating is studied by gravimetric method. The corrosion resistance of the coating is analyzed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy(EIS). The results reveal that the corrosion resistance of epoxy coating is significantly improved by adding the appropriate amount of CNDs, as it can effectively reduce the porosity of the coating and extend the path of corrosive medium penetrating the coating, thus enhancing the corrosion resistance of the coating. After soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 480 h, the low frequency impedance of the 0.15 wt% CNDs/epoxy nanocomposite coating is 1.85x1010 ohm·cm2, which is greatly improved compared with that of the pure epoxy coating (5.43 × 104 O·cm2). The water absorption result shows that the water absorption of the coating with CNDs content of 0.15 wt% is much lower than that of the pure epoxy coating. The corrosion resistance is greatly improved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon nanodots(CNDs)
Corrosion protection
EIS
Epoxy
Water absorption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation illustration of CNDs/epoxy nanocomposite coating.
SEM and TEM images of CNDs, (a) SEM image of CNDs, (b) TEM image of CNDs, (c) SEM image of the pure epoxy coating, (d) 0.05, (e) 0.15, (f) 0.20 CNDs/epoxy.
(a) FT-IR image of CNDs, (b) UV–vis image of CNDs, (c) FT-IR image of pure epoxy and CNDs/epoxy.
XPS image of CNDs (a) the full XPS spectra of CNDs(b) XPS-C1s(c) XPS-N1s.
water absorption of CNDs/epoxy composite coating.

Nyquist and Bode diagrams of immersion test in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution (a), (c), (e) and (g) is the Nyquist diagram of the pure epoxy coating, 0.05, 0.15 and 0.20 CNDs/epoxies respectively; (b), (d), (f) and (h) is the Bode diagram of the pure epoxy coating, 0.05 , 0.15 and 0.20 CNDs/epoxies).
equivalent circuit diagrams of different coatings.
(a) is the charge transfer resistance diagram, (b) is the pore resistance diagram.
A possible protection mechanism, (a) is the pure epoxy coating, (b) is CNDs/epoxy coating, (c) is the partial enlarged view of (b).

head.ce:title: Excellent anti-corrosion performance of epoxy composite coatings filled with novel N-doped carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, 53# Zhengzhou Road, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Safety Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, 53# Zhengzhou Road, Qingdao 266042, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofillers play an important role in the field of anticorrosion. This paper focuses on the study of the anticorrosion performance of a novel N-doped carbon nanodots(CNDs) in epoxy coating. N-doped carbon nanodots(CNDs) with a size of about 100 nm is synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid as carbon source. The chemical structure and morphology of CNDs are studied by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FT-IR), ultraviolet spectrophotometer(UV–vis), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS), scanning electron microscopy(SEM) and transmission electron microscopy(TEM). The water absorption of the coating is studied by gravimetric method. The corrosion resistance of the coating is analyzed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy(EIS). The results reveal that the corrosion resistance of epoxy coating is significantly improved by adding the appropriate amount of CNDs, as it can effectively reduce the porosity of the coating and extend the path of corrosive medium penetrating the coating, thus enhancing the corrosion resistance of the coating. After soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 480 h, the low frequency impedance of the 0.15 wt% CNDs/epoxy nanocomposite coating is 1.85x1010 ohm·cm2, which is greatly improved compared with that of the pure epoxy coating (5.43 × 104 O·cm2). The water absorption result shows that the water absorption of the coating with CNDs content of 0.15 wt% is much lower than that of the pure epoxy coating. The corrosion resistance is greatly improved.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal is widely used in factories, bridges, roads, buildings, and so on. At the same time, metal corrosion is almost unavoidable, causing extremely serious damage to life, safety, and economic development. Therefore, it is of great significance to restrain the metal corrosion rate and prolong its service life. At present, the effective techniques to protect the metal from corrosion are the use of corrosion inhibitors, surface modification, anti-corrosion coating, and electrochemical methods [1]. Anti-corrosion coating is the most economic and effective way. In addition to high-cost performance, a reliable coating should have the following characteristics: good corrosion resistance and durability in corrosion medium, flexibility, good adherence to the substrate, and ease of use [2].

Epoxy resin is widely applied for adhesives and coatings due to its excellent adhesion and corrosion resistance [3]. As thermosetting materials, epoxy exhibits a high degree of crosslinking, which gives them better properties such as high rigidity and high strength. However, the crosslinking structure also makes the epoxy brittle and easy to crack [4]. Meanwhile, the evaporation of solvent will inevitably produce micropores in the curing process of epoxy coating, which reduces the barrier performance and corrosion resistance of the coating, and limits the application of epoxy resin in many fields. Thus, many studies have been carried out by using thermoplastics polymers, liquid rubbers, and nanofillers to improve the properties of epoxy resin [5–9]. Thermoplastics polymers and liquid rubbers can increase the toughness of epoxy, which is of great significance to improve the fracture of the epoxy, but a large amount contents of them, about 5–20 wt%, are needed, causing a great loss of mechanical modulus on epoxy [4,25]. And nanofillers due to their advantages of functionality, small size, high specific surface area, and low manufacturing cost are the essential choice [10].

Graphene and it derivatives, such as carbon nanotubes [11–12], graphene oxide [13–14], functional grapheme [15–17], fullerene [18] provide abundant choices for improving the anti-corrosion performance of polymer composites. Z. Yu et al. [19] modified graphene oxide with nanometer titanium dioxide and found that the dispersibility of TiO2–GO in epoxy resin is better than that of TiO2 and GO, and the impedance of modulus of TiO2-GO/epoxy coating(50 µm) soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl for 90 h is less than 105 O·cm2. H.R. Jeon et al. [20] found that the addition of multi-walled carbon nanotubes resulted in the improvement of coating adhesion, hydrophobic, and corrosion resistance, and the impedance of modulus for soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl for 30 days is less than 107 O·cm2. X. Wang et al. [21] studied the performance of the incorporation of fullerene into the epoxy coating. They found that the fullerene-C60/epoxy coatings exhibited improved mechanical properties, corrosion resistance and excellent durability. The impedance of modulus of the C60/epoxy coating(110 µm) soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl for 200 h is over 1010 O·cm2. It can be seen that due to the two-dimensional geometry, high specific surface area, excellent mechanical properties, and anti-corrosion agent permeability [22–25], GO, carbon nanotube or C60 are used as nano fillers have effectively improved the anticorrosive performance of the coating, but at the same time, their high cost and complex synthesis methods limit their wide application. Recently, A.K. Keerthana et al. [26] revealed that carbon nanodots as a corrosion inhibitor could significantly suppress the oxidation and reduction potential of iron oxide and improve the corrosion resistance of the steel coupons. However, the potential application of N-doped carbon nanodots(CNDs) as nanofillers to improve the corrosion resistance of polymer composites has not been considered. The CNDs has the features of abundant raw materials, low cost, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity [27–29], and its functional groups acylamino, carboxyl and hydroxyl can participate in the curing reaction of epoxy resin, which has the potential to improve the corrosion resistance, its anticorrosion performance as the nanofillers is deserved to be explored.

This paper aims to investigate the resistance corrosion property of epoxy filled by a novel N-doped carbon nanodots(CNDs). The CNDs are prepared via hydrothermal methods using citric acid as carbon sources due to its high yield, ease of fabrication, and low cost, and their reaction product is rich in acylamino, carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups which are beneficial to improve the anti-corrosion performance. To characterize the chemical structure and morphology of CNDs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS), UV–vis, scanning electron microscope(SEM), and transmission electron microscope(TEM) are used. The anti-corrosion performance of the nanocomposite coating is examined by EIS. The result shows that the addition of 0.15 wt% CNDs to epoxy resin can effectively improve the anti-corrosion performance of the coating with the low frequency impedance of 1.85x1010 ohm·cm2 after soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 480 h, which shows the excellent corrosion resistance effect compared with other nanofillers.

Citric acid monohydrate is purchased from MACKLIN. Ethanediamine isobtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The epoxy resin is supplied by Xinhua Resin Manufacturing, Shanghai Coatings Co. The polyamide hardener (amine value = 380 ∼ 420mgKOH/g, viscosity = 2000 ∼ 7000 mPa.s) is procured from MACKLIN. The epoxy coating is prepared with epoxy resin and polyamide hardener. Q235 mild carbon steel(1 cm × 1 cm × 0.15 cm) is purchased from local suppliers. Before the experiments, Q235 mild carbon steel is first polished by the sandpaper of 400, 600, and 1000 grid, and then washed with deionized water(DI) and absolute ethyl alcohol. Deionized water (DI) is used during whole experiments.

The N-doped carbon nanodots(CNDs) is synthesized by a method similar to that reported by Shoujun Zhu et al. [30]. In this paper, the molar ratio of ethylenediamine and citric acid is reduced, and the reaction time is prolonged to promote the dehydration carbonization reaction. Citric acid monohydrate (1.1084 g) is dissolved in 10 ml of DI with ultrasonication, and 354 µl of ethylenediamine is added, then the mixtures are transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) autoclaves. The solution is heated at 200 °C in the oven for 8 h and cooled down to room temperature naturally. After that, the reddish brown solution is obtained. Finally, the N-doped CNDs is obtained by drying the final products at 60 °C under vacuum for 24 h.

Nanocomposite epoxy coating containing 0.05 wt% CNDs is prepared by the following procedure. Polyamide hardener is added into absolute ethyl alcohol stirred evenly. Then 0.05 wt% CNDs is dispersed in the mixture solution by sonication for 30 min [30]. Subsequently, the solution is heated at 30 °C while stirring to evaporate the solvent, and the epoxy resin is added to the mixture, the mass ratio of epoxy resin to curing agent is 1:0.5. After that, Dimethyl benzene and N-butanol are added to the dispersion as a diluent to reduce the viscosity of the mixture, and the paint preparation is completed. The prepared paint is applied by wire rod coater on the mild steel which is cleaned by absolute ethyl alcohol and blow-dried. Finally, The coated substrates are cured at ambient temperature for 12 h and 90 °C for 1 h. The thickness of the coating is about 120 ± 10 µm. So far, the 0.05 wt% CNDs/epoxy nanocomposite coating has been prepared and named as 0.05 CNDs/epoxy. In the meantime, the other contents of CNDs nanocomposite coatings are fabricated in the same way, and named as 0.15 CNDs/epoxy and 0.20 CNDs/epoxy respectively. And the neat epoxy coating is prepared by mixing epoxy resin and polyamide hardener with the same weight ratio as the specimen.

The prepared CNDs and coatings are characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker, Verter-70). The testing spectra are in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumber via a KBr pallet method. The UV–vis (HITACHI U-4100) and X-ray photoelectron spectrum(XPS, AXIS SUPRA) are used to further explore the chemical composition of the CNDs. The micro-morphologies of the synthesis products and nanocomposite coatings are observed by scanning electron microscopy(SEM JSM-7900F) with 5Kv and acceleration voltage is 0.5-30Kv. The micro-morphologies of the synthesis products are further detected by transmission electron microscopy(TEM HT7800). The samples are prepared by placing drops of the diluted CNDs hybrid ethanol suspension onto a copper grid. After drying in the oven, the samples for TEM tests are introduced into the TEM chamber.

The water absorption of the coating is studied by the gravimetric method. The Soptop JA2003 balance(1 µg resolution) is used to measure the mass of each sample at different soaking times. Test and record the initial mass of each sample. During the test, the sample is taken out from the 3.5 wt% NaCl corrosion solution and quickly sucked dry the liquid on the surface of the sample with filter paper before weighing. Each sample is measured three times and the average value is taken.

To study the corrosion resistance properties of the coating in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, the conventional three-electrode system of an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) is used. The saturated calomel electrode is used as reference electrode, the platinum plate electrode(2 cm × 2 cm) is used as counter electrode, and the epoxy-coated steel specimen is used as working electrode. After the electrochemical system achieves steady open circuit potential, the EIS tests are proceeded under the range of 105 and 10-2 Hz with an AC amplitude of 10mv. The tests are performed under ambient temperature. The EIS data is analyzed by ZView software. Fig. 1

The size and morphology of CNDs are characterized by SEM and TEM. The SEM and TEM image showed that the CNDs has a nearly spherical shape, shown in Fig. 2 . From TEM image, it can be seen that the size of CNDs are distribution of about 100 nm, shown in Fig. 2(b).

Fig. 2(c-f) are the morphology images of the pure epoxy coating, 0.05 CNDs/epoxy, 0.15 CNDs/epoxy and 0.20 CNDs/epoxy respectively. It can be seen that the CNDs uniform disperses in 0.05 CNDs/epoxy and 0.15 CNDs/epoxy as shown in Fig. 2(d) and (e), but it agglomerates in 0.20 CNDs/epoxy as shown in Fif.1(f).

The chemical structure of CNDs is characterized by FT-IR and UV–Vis. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), it shows that the absorption peak at 3400 cm−1 for CNDs is assigned to –OH bending stretching vibration and –NH stretching vibrations of amine group. The weak peaks between 2965 cm−1 and 2961 cm−1 are attributed to the C-H bond stretching vibration. The sharp peak at 1656 cm−1 corresponds to acylamino N-C = O stretching vibration, and 1552 cm−1 is classified in amine N-H bond deformation vibration. And the middle strong peak at 1389 cm−1 is assigned to C-H bond. Furthermore, the absorption peaks at 1047 cm−1 and 780 cm−1 belong to C-N stretching vibrations and N-H out-of-plan vibration, respectively. The UV–Vis spectrum of CNDs shown in Fig. 3(b) indicates that there are two absorption peaks at 221 nm and 345 nm, which are attributed to the π–π* transition of the aliphatic sp2 domains and n-π* electronic transition of C = O bonds, respectively.

Fig. 3(c) shows the FT-IR image of pure epoxy and CNDs/epoxy. It can be seen that the characteristic peaks of amide group of N-H(at 3358 cm−1, 1608 cm−1, 1509 cm−1 and 829 cm−1) in the CNDs/epoxy has red-shifted compared with the peaks of N-H(at 3426 cm−1, 1610 cm−1,1510 cm−1 and 831 cm−1), C = O(at 1655 cm−1) and C-N(at 1040 cm−1) in the pure epoxy. It can be inferred that the red-shifted belongs to the formation of hydrogen bonds in CNDs.

The XPS of the CNDs is shown in Fig. 4 . From the full XPS spectra of CNDs as shown in Fig. 4(a), it can be seen that the pronounced peaks at 284.8ev, 399.4ev, and 531ev are classified in C1s, N1s, and O1s, and the content of C, N, and O elements are 76.31at.%, 9.75at.%, and 13.94at.% respectively. Fig. 4(b) shows the high-resolution spectra of the C1s reign, and it can be divided into three peaks at the binding energy of 284.6ev, 286.2ev, and 288.2ev which are corresponding to C-C/C = C, C-N, and C = O respectively. And the carbon atom at 284.6ev is the form of graphitic structures. The high-resolution N1s spectrum of CNDs is shown in Fig. 4.(c), two peaks centered at 399.5ev and 400.9ev are associated with C-N-C and C-N. The formation of C-N-C is attributed to the reaction of citric acid and ethylenediamine. The above results indicates that CNDs has been synthesized successfully.

Water absorption is an important factor to measure the anti-corrosion performance and service life of the coating [1,15,31]. The prepared paintings are coated on the polytetrafluoroethylene board, and after curing the coatings are removed, then the coatings are soaked in the water.

Fig. 5 shows the water absorption(φw) curves of epoxy coatings with different content of CNDs soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl for different times. The water absorption(φw) of coating is calculated by the following equation[1,31]:
(1)
ϕ W
=
m t
-
m 0
m 0
× 100 %
Where mt is the mass of specimen at time t, m0 is the mass of specimen before immersion and φw is the water absorption at time t.

It can be seen that all coatings show a similar trend, and two distinct stages are presented during the water absorption process. In the first stage, the absorption of water increases rapidly, and then it slows down, the absorption process enters the second stage which remains stable. As can be seen in Fig. 5, In 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, the 0.05 and 0.15 CNDs/epoxies get saturation after soaking for 72 h, the 0.20 CNDs/epoxy gets saturation after soaking for 144 h and the pure epoxy coating get saturation after soaking for 120 h. For the pure epoxy coating, 0.05, 0.15 and 0.20 CNDs/epoxies, the values of saturation water absorption are around 2.5%, 1.3%, 2.1% and 5.5% respectively.

Compared with the pure epoxy coating, the curves of 0.05 and 0.15 CNDs/epoxies show that the rising amplitudes for both at the stable stage are lower than that of the pure epoxy coating. But for 0.20 CNDs/epoxy, it shows the fastest rising rate in the first stage, and it also has the highest water absorption in the stable stage in all the coatings.

The results show that the addition of CNDs has a great influence on the water absorption of the coating. Initially, the water absorption decreases with the addition of CNDs, but with the increase of adding amount it increases. And from the experimental results, it indicates that the addition of CNDs can effectively decrease the saturation of water absorption of the coating compared with the pure epoxy coating. When the addition of CNDs is no more than 0.15 wt%, and the addition amount of 0.05 wt% shows the best performance, but when the addition amount is more than 0.15 wt%, the addition of CNDs harms the water-resistance of the coating. And it also can be seen that the 0.05 wt% and 0.15 wt% CNDs shorten the rising time to get saturation, but the addition amount of 0.20 wt% CNDs extends the time. The results indicate that the improved barrier performance is achieved by adding 0.05 wt% and 0.15 wt% CNDs into epoxy coating.

The corrosion protection performance of the pure epoxy coating, 0.05, 0.15, and 0.20 CNDs/epoxies in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution is investigated via the electrochemical impedance technique. The test area is 1 × 1 cm2. The result of EIS experiments are displayed by Nyquist and Bode plot for coatings at different immersion time, as shown in Fig. 6 .

In general, the corrosion process can be divided into two stages. In the first stage, the corrosive medium is penetrating the coating, and if there are micro-holes, cracks or other defects in the coating, the process will be greatly accelerated. In this stage, the EIS exhibits one time constant, the coating resistance decreases obviously, and the corrosion medium has not reached the metal substrate. In the second stage, the corrosive medium reaches the metal substrate, and the corrosion reaction takes place on the metal substrate that the second time constant appears, and the coating gradually degenerates.

For the pure epoxy coating, as shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b), the Nyquist diagram displays two capacitive loops under all immersion time from 24 h to 480 h, which exhibits that there has two-time constants, and it indicated that the corrosion medium has penetrated the coating and taken reaction with the metal substrate just after soaking 24 h. In the Bode diagram, phase angle plots shows two peaks for all immersion time also verified this point, and from the modulus of low-frequency impedance(|Z|f=0.01Hz), it can be seen that when the immersion time increases from 24 h to 120 h, the |Z|f=0.01Hz deceased from |Z|f=0.01Hz =
3.07 ×
10
6
o h m ∙
cm
2
to 8.76 × 105 O·cm2 that is lower than 106 O·cm2, and it further decreases to 5.43 × 104 O·cm2 at 480 h, indicating the serious degradation of the coating. The coating breakpoint frequency (the frequency for phase angle at 45°) as shown in Fig. 6(b) reflects the delamination area of the coating. According to the formula as following, the higher fb is, the more delamination area at the interface of metal and coating is [1]:
(2)
f b
=
A t
ρ ε
ε 0
2
A 0

Where fb is the breakpoint frequency, At is the delamination area, A0 is the total area of the sample, ρ is resistivity of the coating, ε is the dielectric constant of water in coating, and ε0 is the vacuum permittivity.

For the pure epoxy coating, the fb rises from
6.81 ×
103Hz at 24 h to 2.67 × 104Hz at 240 h. And with the immersion time goes on, the fb moves to a higher value that is out of our measurement frequency.

For 0.05 CNDs/epoxy, as can be seen in Fig. 6(c) and (d), the Nyquist diagram also shows two capacitive loops at the soaking time from 24 h to 480 h, and the phase angle plots displays two peaks at the same time, which indicates that there are two-time constants under all immersion time. The modulus plots show that the value of low-frequency impedance (|Z|f=0.01Hz) decreases from 1.45 × 107 O·cm2 to 9.00 × 104 O·cm2 with the soaking time increases from 24 h to 480 h which is better than the pure epoxy coating. The breakpoint frequency fb rises from 3.83 × 101Hz at 24 h to 2.15 × 104Hz at 240 h and the fb at 480 h is out of our measurement, which is lower than that of the pure epoxy coating. The improvement of anti-corrosion property may be due to the hydrogen and covalent bond formed between CNDs, which will be discussed in section 3.3. And the SEM image of this coating(Fig. 2(c)) shows that CNDs is evenly dispersed in epoxy resin. The results present the adding of CNDs into epoxy is helpful to improve the anticorrosion property, but the appearance of two-time constants also indicates the anticorrosion effect is limited for this low addition amount, and the higher amount should be further explored.

For 0.15 CNDs/epoxy, the Nyquist diagram only shows one capacitive loop during 24 h to 480 h of immersion as shown in Fig. 6(e) and (f), which indicates the coating is not penetrated. At 24 h of immersion, the value of |Z|f=0.01Hz is about 9.92 × 1010 O·cm2, and its impedance modulus is the highest value in this experiment. After soaking for 480 h, the |Z|f=0.01Hz value is still at 1.85 × 1010 O·cm2, and the |Z|f=0.01Hz value of 0.15 CNDs/epoxy is much higher than that of the pure epoxy and 0.05 CNDs/epoxy. The anticorrosion effect is greatly improved. The breakpoint frequency of fb that rises slowly from 1.78 × 10-2Hz at 24 h to 2.15 × 10-2Hz at 480 h also shows the high anticorrosion performance, which is significantly lower than that of the pure epoxy and 0.05 CNDs/epoxy. Fig. 2(d) and (e) show that both of the 0.05 wt% and 0.15 wt% CNDs uniformly disperses in the epoxy resin, but 0.15 wt% CNDs has a higher density, and more content of CNDs in epoxy significantly enhanced the anticorrosive properties of epoxy coating.

For 0.20 CNDs/epoxy, shown in Fig. 6(g) and (h), the Nyquist diagram displays two loops within 24 h to 480 h of immersion. As the immersion time increases, the arc diameter first increases and then decreases, indicating that the protective performance of the coating also increases first and then decreases, which can be proved by the change of low-frequency impedance modulus (|Z|f=0.01Hz). At beginning, the |Z|f=0.01Hz of 0.20 CNDs/epoxy is lower than that of the measurement of the epoxy coating, and then it is gradually rising from 9.68 × 105 O·cm2 to 1.02 × 107 O·cm2 with the immersion time increases from 24 h to 240 h. After soaking for 480 h, |Z|f=0.01Hz of 0.20 CNDs/epoxy decreases to 6.10 × 105 O·cm2. In addition to the value at 24 h, |Z|f=0.01Hz of 0.20 CNDs/epoxy is much higher than that gauged of the epoxy and 0.05 CNDs/epoxy during 120 h to 480 h. And the value of |Z|f=0.01Hz is better than pure epoxy and 0.05 CNDs/epoxy, but is about 3 to 5 order magnitude lower than that of 0.15 CNDs/epoxy. Fig. 6(h) exhibits that the breakpoint frequency fb decreases from 8.25 × 103Hz at 24 h to 3.16 × 103Hz at 240 h, and then rises to 8.25 × 103Hz at 480 h. For fb of 480 h immersion time, it is lower than that of the pure epoxy and 0.05 CNDs/epoxy, but is much higher than 0.15 CNDs/epoxy. The decrease of the anti-corrosion performance may be caused by the agglomeration of CNDs in epoxy resins shown in Fig. 2(f).

Obviously, the addition of CNDs has improved the corrosion resistance of the coating. When the addition amounts of CNDs is no more than 0.15 wt%, corrosion resistance of the epoxy increases with the increase of CNDs, but when the amount of CNDs is more than 0.15 wt%, the addition of CNDs has an adverse effect on the anti-corrosion performance of the coating. In summary, the 0.15 CNDs/epoxy presents the best anti-corrosion property.

In order to better understand the corrosion process, Fig. 7 manifests the equivalent circuit model of the coating corrosion test. In Fig. 7, Rs, Rcoat, and Rct are the solution resistance, coating pore resistance, and charge transfer resistance, respectively. The frequency response characteristics of solid electrode electric double-layer capacitor are different from that of pure capacitor, so constant phase angle element CPE is introduced. The capacitance of coating is expressed by CPEc, and the double-layer capacitance formed by the substrate-coating interface is represented by CPEdl. Zw is Warburg impedance, caused by the diffusion of corrosive medium in the coating. The Nyquist diagram of the pure epoxy coating(shown in Fig. 6(a)) and 0.20 CNDs/epoxy nanocomposite(shown in Fig. 6(g)) coating show two capacitive loops, and in the Bode diagram(Fig. 6(b) and Fig. 6(h)) the diffusion impedance appears in the intermediate frequency region, so they are fitted with the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 7(b). For 0.05 CNDs/epoxy(shown in Fig. 6(c)), two capacitive arcs are also observed, but without diffusion impedance, and the circuit in Fig. 7(a) is used. For 0.15 CNDs/epoxy, only one semicircle shows, and the equivalent circuit in Fig. 7(c) can be used.

Fig. 8 (a) shows the charge transfer resistance (Rct) for different nanocomposite coatings. As the increase of immersion time, it can be seen that the charge transfer resistance(Rct) of the pure epoxy coating drops drastically from 2.09 × 106 O·cm2 to 1.48 × 105 O·cm2, and for 0.05 CNDs/epoxy the charge transfer resistance (Rct) decreases from 9.88 × 106 O·cm2 to 2.47 × 105 O·cm2. Comparing with the pure epoxy coating, the performance of anti-corrosion is improved, but for 0.20 CNDs/epoxy, the charge resistance is 1.93 × 106 O·cm2 at the beginning, and then it increases to 1.03 × 107 O·cm2 at 240 h, and finally decreased to 3.22 × 105 O·cm2. The results show the addition of CNDs plays a role in inhibiting the electron transfer, which is helpful to corrosion prevention, but the shown of Rct also indicates that the coating corrosion process has entered into the second stage and the metal substrate has been corroded. For 0.15 CNDs/epoxy, the Rct does not appear during the experiment, indicating that the corrosion reaction has not occurred on the metal substrate. Revealing that the addition of 0.15 wt% CNDs shows the best anti-corrosion effect.

The pore resistance Rcoat can be used to reflect the barrier performance of the coating, it can be combined with the coating thickness L (120 µm), the test area of coating A(10-4m2) and the conductance of 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at 25 °C K(0.01S/m) to calculate coating porosity, which reflects the protective performance of the coating. The equation is as following [32–34]:
(3)
coating =
coating
r e s i s tan c e
w i t h o u t
a n y
p o r e s
coating
r e s i s tan c e
d e r i v e d
b y
E I S
=
L
AK
R
coat
The variation of Rcoat is shown in Fig. 7(b), derived by fitting EIS data with equivalent electrical circuit.

As shown in Fig. 9 , the porosity of the pure epoxy coating increases from 4.96 × 10-4cm−2 to 2.09 × 10-3cm−2 after soaking for 480 h, as for 0.05 CNDs/epoxy, it increases from 1.52 × 10-5cm−2 to 2.03 × 10-3cm−2. Its porosity is lower than that of the pure epoxy coating, indicating that the addition of CNDs has reduced the porosity and improved the corrosion resistance of the coating. For 0.15 CNDs/epoxy, the porosity increases slowly from 9.81 × 10-10cm−2 to 6.23 × 10-9cm−2 after soaking for 480 h. Compared to 0.05 CNDs/epoxy, its porosity is significantly lower, due to the increased amount of CNDs and evenly dispersed in the epoxy resin. For 0.20 CNDs/epoxy, the coating porosity drops first and then increases. The decrease of the porosity may result from the formation of hydrogen and covalent bond. Then with the increases of the immersion time, the coating is continuously attacked by corrosive medium and its porosity finally rises to 1.94 × 10-3cm−2, which is similar to the pure epoxy and 0.05 CNDs/epoxy. But it is much higher than that of 0.15 CNDs/epoxy, which may be caused by the agglomeration of the CNDs in epoxy resin.

The results reveal that the porosity of 0.15 CNDs/epoxy is the lowest, and is about 5 to 6 order magnitude lower than that of the other coatings in this study. Indicating that the addition of CNDs can improve the anti-corrosion performance of the coating and the coating with 0.15 wt%. CNDs shows the best corrosion resistance.

To further understand the anti-corrosion performance of the coating, a possible protection mechanism is proposed, as shown in Fig. 10 . Metal corrosion includes oxidation and reduction processes, and the main corrosive media is water and oxygen. For pure epoxy coating, water and oxygen can rapidly penetrate the coating to the metal substrate through the inherent pores channels of the coating. The addition of CNDs can effectively prevent the penetration of corrosive media, so as to achieve remarkable anti-corrosion effects.

The dispersibility of nanoparticles and their bonding force with epoxy resin are two key factors affecting the coating anti-corrosion performance. CNDs has a large number of functional groups on the surface, such as carboxyl, amino and amide groups. Among them, the amide group could participate in the curing process of the epoxy resin, as shown in Fig. 10, to form covalent bond, which is conducive to enhancing the bonding force between the nanoparticles and the epoxy resin. Carboxyl, amino and amide groups in CNDs can also form hydrogen and covalent bond with each other, as discussed in section 3.1.2 shown in Fig. 3(c), so that the addition of CNDs nanoparticles can block the penetration of the corrosive medium into the epoxy resin, delay the degradation of the epoxy resin, improve the anti-corrosion performance of the epoxy resin, reduce the corrosion speed, and protect the metal substrate. Furthermore, CNDs particles located on both sides of the inherent pore channels of the epoxy resin can be combined with each other, reducing the inherent pore channels of the epoxy resin which can explain the change in the porosity of the coating.

In this work, CNDs is synthesized by citric acid and ethylenediamine. It is added to epoxy resin to prepare CNDs/epoxy nanocomposite coating. The water absorption test and EIS results show that, compared with the pure epoxy coating, the amount of 0.15 wt% CNDs significantly improves the anti-corrosion performance of the coating. The 0.15 CNDs/epoxy has a lower water absorption saturation, a higher low-frequency impedance modulus and a lower coating porosity compared with the pure epoxy coating, which may be attributed to the hydrogen and covalent bond formed between CNDs each other as well as CNDs and epoxy resin. This work provides a new choice for improving the anti-corrosive performance of the epoxy coating.

Shaoxiang Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Feifan Du: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis. Yanxu Lin: Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Project administration. Yu Guan: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. WenJuan Qu: Formal analysis, Validation. Jiaji Cheng: Formal analysis, Validation. Dong Wang: Formal analysis, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (NO. ZR2020QE134), Major Science and Technology Innovation Program of Shandong Province (NO. 2019JZZY010821), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 51806113).
The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author. The data are not publicly available due to privacy or ethical restrictions
